mnemonics.title: Interval Scheduling Greedy Order
mnemonics.content: FISH: Finish Earliest, Start Here - Always pick the interval that finishes earliest to leave room for more selections.

mnemonics.title: Fractional Knapsack Order
mnemonics.content: VWR: Value Weight Ratio - Sort by ratio descending, take highest ratio items first, fraction last item if needed.

mnemonics.title: Greedy Success Conditions
mnemonics.content: GPS: Greedy choice Property + Substructure = Optimal solution. Both must hold for greedy to work.

acronyms.expansion: Interval Scheduling Problem - maximize non-overlapping intervals
acronyms.usage: When scheduling resources like meeting rooms or classrooms

acronyms.expansion: Fractional Knapsack Problem - maximize value with fractional items
acronyms.usage: When items are divisible like liquids, grains, or budget allocation

acronyms.expansion: Earliest Finish Time - greedy choice for interval scheduling
acronyms.usage: Select interval with smallest finish time first

acronyms.expansion: Value-to-Weight Ratio - greedy criterion for knapsack
acronyms.usage: Sort items by value/weight in descending order

visualTips.visualization: Think of timelines as horizontal bars. The greedy approach is like placing books on a shelf - always put the book that ends earliest, so more books can fit to its right.

Imagine a jar filled with different colored sands (items) of different values. You want maximum value in the jar - take the most valuable colored sand first (highest ratio), then mix in the next valuable, and finally fill gaps with the last one.
Think of climbing a mountain by always taking the steepest upward path - if this local choice leads to the peak, the problem has greedy choice property.
Picture fence posts where intervals are ropes between posts. Two intervals are compatible if their ropes don't cross - one ends before the other begins.

analogies.analogy: Movie scheduling at a cinema - you want to watch maximum shows. Always pick the show that ends earliest so you have time for more shows later.
Filling a suitcase for a trip with valuable souvenirs that can be broken (fractioned). You take whole valuable items first, then break the last item to fill remaining space.

Greedy is like making the best immediate decision without planning ahead (like taking the stairs). Dynamic programming is like planning the entire route (like using an elevator with floor-by-floor strategy).
0/1 is like packing whole items in a suitcase - once an item is in, it stays. Fractional is like pouring different liquids into a container - you can fill gaps with partial amounts.
